subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear definition of urban stress linked directly to IB syllabus content
Precise geographic context with population, voivodeship, and coastal location
Well-justified feasibility of primary data collection at ten identified sites
weaknesses: Research question phrased too broadly with loosely defined operational variables
Map lacks grid lines or coordinates and has a cramped legend
Terms in hypotheses (e.g., âdifferent forms of securityâ) need clearer operationalization
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student formulates a geographical question linked appropriately to CPTED theory with three testable hypotheses, placing performance solidly in band 2. Geographic context is precise (band 3), but the broad wording of the question and inconsistencies in cartographic conventions limit full marks. Overall performance across strands warrants 2 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Comprehensive CPTED theory explanation underpinning hypotheses
Clear description of field methods (dates, sites, survey) linked to research question
Appropriate use of Spearmanâs rank aligning with ordinal data
weaknesses: Lacks justification for chosen 300 m radius and 15 min counts
Sampling strategy and sample size not justified for reliability
Secondary image usage not explained within methods
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are described with clear relevance to the question and correctly applied to yield primary data, placing performance in band 2. Explanations remain procedural, lacking depth in theoretical alignment and methodological justification, so a mid-band score of 2 is appropriate.

strengths: Use of varied presentation techniques (tables, scatter plots, hand-drawn maps)
Clear rationale for ordinal measurement scales per 1000 inhabitants
weaknesses: Frequent errors in labelling, conventions, and figure numbering
Overlap in category descriptions reduces scoring consistency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While data are relevant and presentation methods appropriate (lower end of band 3â4), frequent convention lapses and thin datasets restrict treatment to partial analysis. This performance aligns with the upper edge of band 1â2, justifying 2 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and statistical techniques (Spearmanâs rank, lines of best fit)
Discussion of trends and anomalies linked to CPTED theory
Thorough anomaly interpretation drawing on site characteristics
weaknesses: No significance testing or confidence intervals applied
Interpretations sometimes speculative and lack explicit thresholds for correlation strength
Analytical gaps: no composite urban stress index, survey results not fully integrated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis goes beyond basic description with appropriate statistical techniques and theory linking (band 3â4), but lacks depth in significance testing and completeness. The presence of meaningful interpretation and anomaly discussion merits 3 marks.

strengths: Conclusion summarises which hypotheses were supported
References key anomalies identified in the analysis
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative evidence and reflection on data reliability
Minimal discussion of overall strengths or limitations of findings
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: A partially supported conclusion is present (band 1), as it states which hypotheses hold but fails to cite statistical results or critique reliability. This corresponds to 1 mark.

strengths: Lists anomalies and mentions teacher advice for improvements
weaknesses: Superficial outline of strengths and weaknesses without systematic analysis
Improvement suggestions are general without impact explanations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation lists some strengths/weaknesses and suggests improvements but remains superficial with no detailed impact analysis, fitting band 1 and warranting 1 mark.

strandSelections.reasoning: A hand-drawn site map and Poland location inset are provided, but cartographic conventions are inconsistent: a north arrow and scale are present, yet the key is cramped, some labels are hard to read and no grid or co-ordinates are supplied. Detail is acceptable but not fully clear, so the work fits the middle band.

The research question is clearly geographical, focused on the relationship between crime rate and urban stress in GdaÅsk, and it sits explicitly within Option G of the syllabus. The student links the question to CPTED theory and derives three testable hypotheses.  However, the wording of the question is rather broad (âto what extentâ) and the operational variables (crime rate/urban stress) are only loosely defined, so the link to theory is evident but not âclearâ enough to facilitate fully-precise hypothesis formulation. Hence a solid, but not top, performance for this strand.

Tables, hand-drawn maps and scatter plots with trend lines are appropriate and varied. While axes are sometimes mis-labelled and the graphics are basic, they are serviceable and help the reader follow the analysis. This fits the lower end of the 3â4 band rather than the basic band.

Techniques (field counts, simple Likert survey, secondary crime data, scatter plots) are correctly applied and yield primary data from 10 sites, yet the sample is small and heavily qualitative so data quality and reliability are limited. That aligns with âcorrectly used but with minimal data collection.â

The student goes beyond description by calculating Spearmanâs rank for five variables and plotting lines of best fit, demonstrating appropriate descriptive and statistical techniques. However, no significance testing or confidence limits are applied, so the work does not reach the 5â6 band.

Trends and anomalies are discussed for every graph, and links to CPTED theory are attempted. Yet explanations are often speculative, occasionally contradictory, and not always well-supported by the limited data set, preventing achievement of the 5â6 or 7â8 bands.

The context section locates GdaÅsk precisely (population, voivodeship, Baltic coast) and justifies the choice in terms of contrasting crime patterns that are feasible to measure. Ten survey sites are identified and later mapped, demonstrating realistic scope for primary data collection. The level of locational specificity comfortably meets the highest descriptor.

The evaluation lists several anomalies and mentions advice from the teacher, but strengths and weaknesses of methods (e.g., sampling size, subjectivity, time of day) are not systematically analysed. Depth is superficial, corresponding to band 1.

All collected data relate to crime and urban stress, but the dataset is thin (only 10 points, many subjective scales) and some variables such as âquality of infrastructureâ are highly interpretive. This allows only partial analysis, matching the upper edge of the 1â2 band.

A single paragraph conclusion summarises which hypotheses were supported and references anomalies, but support is partial; no quantitative evidence is cited and there is little reflection on overall reliability. This aligns with a âpartially supported conclusion.â

Important analytical gaps remain: only bivariate relationships are tested, the survey results are not statistically integrated, and no composite index of urban stress is produced. Consequently, the analysis is descriptive with noticeable omissions, matching the 1â2 band.

Methods are described (date, time-frame, 10 sites, 300 m radius, 15-minute counts, survey question) and linked to each hypothesis, showing relevance. Nonetheless, explanations remain brief and largely procedural; there is little discussion of why variables or scales were chosen or how CPTED underpinned the design. This places the work in the âclear but not comprehensiveâ band.

There are frequent convention errors: inconsistent figure numbering, missing units on several axes, hand-drawn maps without scale bars, and algebraic symbols (\overline{c}) left unresolved. Occasional correctness is present but lapses are common, so the strand remains in the 1â2 range.

Suggested improvements are limited to general ideas such as increasing precision or expanding surveys; there is no detailed explanation of how changes would influence results. This meets only the descriptor for âsuperficial improvement suggestions.â

annotations.comment: The theoretical explanation of CPTED is comprehensive and clearly underpins the hypotheses, linking environmental design principles to measurable urban variables.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The anecdotal explanation for anomalies (site 6 vs site 1) is insightful, but quantifying the deviation (e.g., residuals from trend line) would strengthen the interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The research question, âTo what extent does the crime rate have an effect on urban stressâ¦?â, is phrased too broadly. The operational definitions of âcrime rateâ and âurban stressâ should be clarified to ensure precise, testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear definition of urban stress linked directly to IB syllabus content, situating the investigation within the context of urban environments and social stresses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Geographic context is precise: population, density, voivodeship, and coastal location are given. This supports feasibility of primary data collection at defined sites in GdaÅsk.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The map (Figure 1) lacks grid lines or coordinate references and has a cramped legend. Enhancing these conventions would improve spatial clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypotheses are clearly stated and linked to CPTED principles, but terms like âdifferent forms of securityâ should be operationalized to specify which security measures are measured.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The methods section lists dates, durations, and radii for site visits but lacks justification for the chosen 300 m radius and 15 min counts. Explain why these parameters best test the hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Incorporating a random street survey enriches primary data, but the sampling strategy and sample size (five per site) should be justified to ensure reliability and representativeness.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The rubric table for scoring site characteristics is detailed, but several descriptions overlap (e.g., graffiti vs. infrastructure), reducing scoring consistency. Clarify category boundaries.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The rationale explaining why higher scale points correspond to lower crime probability is clear and effectively justifies the chosen ordinal measurement system.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The algebraic symbols $$\overline{c}$$ appear unresolved in the ratings table, causing confusion. Replace or define this notation clearly.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The crime-rate-to-scale classification is well structured, offering clear intervals for number of offences. This supports consistency in secondary data treatment.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 6 lacks a clear legend and the resolution makes district labels hard to read. Improving clarity and adding a scale bar would enhance spatial interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Creating separate scales per 1000 inhabitants allows more nuanced comparison between districts, showing sensitivity to differences in population size.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Spearmanâs rank is appropriate for ordinal data, but the choice over Pearson should be justified in terms of data distribution and measurement scales.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The use of Spearmanâs coefficient aligns well with ordinal scales defined. This underpins the statistical validity of the correlation analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Describing the positive correlation in Figure 9 as âstrongâ requires consistent thresholds (e.g., |r_s| > 0.5). Explicitly state your criteria for interpreting correlation strength.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Interpretation of $$r_s=-0.4619$$ in Figure 12 should clarify axis inversion if safety replaces crime, ensuring consistency in graphical conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Photographic evidence at site 8 provides context but remains anecdotal. Integrate photos with quantitative data trends to support broader analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Discussion of anomalies in Figures 12 and 13 is thorough, linking outliers to other site characteristics and theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The narrative listing all site characteristics in one paragraph is dense. Consider synthesizing key variables into a composite index to clarify relative influence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Site photos are numerous but lack accompanying analytical commentary in the main text, limiting their interpretive value.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The Google-sourced image at site 8 lacks explanation in methods. Clarify how secondary images complement primary observations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The conclusion summarizes which hypotheses were confirmed but lacks quantitative evidence and reflection on data reliability. Include explicit reference to key statistical results.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion
